text:
Interesting approach. I think this is the key aspect:
>  the identity of individual expressions is tracked using tags
Essentially you have objects with identity and corresponding mini-views in the editor. It's clever how this is embedded in a typical text editing experience using tags. Would definitely play around with it more once you publish 'getting started' or similar.

text: One of the Problems I see with answering it is how to *measure* if learning with kids "works" or doesn't.  In Paperts book Mindstorms he explains some interesting aspects of his vision. Later in the book he also has this example of Samba school as an example of a learning environment. I think, or at least it seems to me that she took him too "literal" or too constructivist - dogmatic. Which is never a good idea. I I still think Papert and his work have valuable lessons in them. But for her as for a very very very large part of society its just too much of a leap from existing culture.

text: Paperts words in Mindstorms regarding - Computer Cultures are real. But its not limited to Computers current "Mainstream" Civilization Cultures does quite a bad job at acknowledging its biases towards certain tendencies. So a bias towards explanation might not be easily spotted but still be there. I will just point to some effort which does also lean towards more self-directed learning. and these are SOLES such as proposed by Sugata Mitra: <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Self_Organised_Learning_Environment>

attachments.title: Self Organised Learning Environment
attachments.text: A Self Organized Learning Environment (SOLE) is a program designed to support self-directed education. Sugata Mitra, an education scientist, first popularized the term in 1999, referencing an approach he developed following his Hole in the Wall experiments. Mitra's experiments demonstrated that groups of kids could learn to navigate computers and the internet by themselves, and "research since then has continued to support his startling conclusion that groups of children, with access to the Internet, can learn almost anything by themselves." Starting in 2014, he's worked with and through the School in the Cloud project to support the development of SOLEs around the world, adding "Granny" mentors and Big Questions as key components of such programs.

text:
Core is an interesting upcoming game building tool.  This has the most information I've seen about what the actual creation tools might look like.  I'd like to see the scripting capabilities.  Although I'm guessing its similar to a slightly more generic version of Overwatch's Workshop scripting they haven't shown anything.  This is funny to me because they made a lot of choices that were clearly stickier\more fun for users  (pre-built game modes, heavy kitbashing), but aren't as "pure" for a technical\engineering standpoint
<https://www.theverge.com/2020/3/2/21158189/core-free-game-creation-tool-pc-youtube-twitch>

attachments.title: Core is a new platform aiming to be the YouTube of game development
attachments.text: Opening up game creation to more people.
attachments.fallback: The Verge: Core is a new platform aiming to be the YouTube of game development

text: Hard to tell just from the screenshots, but it seems as though you're stuck with a very Fortnite/Apex/Overwatch-esq art style. I wonder if that ends up being a plus or a minus.

text: Even Dreams seems to be struggling a bit with that, in that it offers seemingly much broader art styles than typical mod tools / editors, but still much narrower styles than going all the way to something like Unity.

text:
There are probably a handful of sweet spots along that spectrum. Would Minecraft have been a touch less viral if it didn't force all your creations to largely conform to its unique aesthetic? Probably.

Too bad Core doesn't have a unique aesthetic (yet).

text:
<@UU261JPCH> I was unaware of GDevelop. Thanks for the link.

Blueprints are a fairly full features programming system. Full AAA games can be largely (but not completely) made in blueprints without needing to program in C++. Generally, I see teams use blueprints for rapid prototyping and for creating higher level abstractions to be used by non-programmers, such as technical artists or technical game designers.

<@UMUJX89KL> let's definitely chat more.

elements.text:
I was unaware of GDevelop. Thanks for the link.

Blueprints are a fairly full features programming system. Full AAA games can be largely (but not completely) made in blueprints without needing to program in C++. Generally, I see teams use blueprints for rapid prototyping and for creating higher level abstractions to be used by non-programmers, such as technical artists or technical game designers.
let's definitely chat more.

text:
I am very lazy and generally prefer things which are Easy in the Simple vs Easy Rich Hickey sense. Close to hand, fast to get going with, eventual catastrophes :grinning:

I also like tools that know their scope, integrate well with others, and leave their connections "raw". My worst nightmare is things that are highly configurable but in a "closed" way, e.g. a lot of creative software like Photoshop, Blender, Unreal Engine etc.

For example, in Sublime Text, the Build command will basically just execute a string you provide as a command. This is so much more appealing to me to than trying to configure Eclipse. The "connection" in Sublime Text is more "raw".
